headline: BYD Blocked, Proton Shielded, B40 Priced Out — Who Wins?
context: MITI's conditions for BYD's Tanjung Malim EV plant — 80% export and an RM200K price floor — stalled the world's largest EV maker's Malaysia plans, shielding Proton (49.9% Geely) and Perodua (25.2% Daihatsu) and pricing B40 out of the EV transition.

cards.big: MITI set an RM200K floor and 80% export rule. BYD walked. Thailand didn't.
cards.sub: Both Proton (49.9% Geely) and Perodua (25.2% Daihatsu) are shielded. Neither is wholly Malaysian-owned.

cards.big: BYD Dolphin sells at RM100K. MITI's floor is RM200K. B40 median income: RM4,588/month.
cards.sub: Only 5,624 public chargers installed — 56% of target. High-rise residents can't charge at home.

cards.big: Proton had 43 years of tariffs and subsidies. It still can't compete on price.
cards.sub: Every party — BN, PH, PN — has extended the wall. 700,000 jobs includes 53,000 aftermarket shops.

cards.big: Thailand gave BYD zero export quotas and zero price floors. Production started 2024.
cards.sub: Indonesia and India attach conditions too — but transparent, time-bound, and linked to capability-building.

cards.big: After 43 years of protection and zero exit plan, who is the wall actually for?
Like building a wall around a garden that stopped growing 20 years ago — the wall keeps competitors out, but it also keeps the garden from getting sunlight
cards.sub: 43 years of tariff protection have not produced a globally competitive national car; they have produced higher prices for everyone else

cards.big: 700,000 jobs deserve an honest transition — not an indefinite regressive tax on the bottom 40%.
